currentState: Chapter 12 turns this relationship from old wound into live philosophical split. They still do not share a room, but Kel'Thuzad now defines himself against Antonidas explicitly, framing the ban on necromancy as moral retreat where Antonidas would have called it civilization.
powerDynamic: They remain scholarly peers in lineage, but chapter 12 clarifies the real break: Antonidas represents the institution willing to stop at bodies; Kel'Thuzad represents the mind that treats that stop as cowardice. The dead colleague now argues with the absent teacher through Jaina.

physicalDynamics.pattern: No shared physical scene on page. The chapter renders the relationship through Antonidas's sending-stone silence and the scale of the machine Kel'Thuzad has already built.

nameUsage.antonidasHearsKelThuzad: The name lands with no ornament and produces the chapter's most telling silence.
nameUsage.kelthuzadAsInstitutionalMemory: He is spoken of not as rumor anymore but as a specific former Kirin Tor mind with history attached to Antonidas.

history.event: Chapter 8 establishes openly that Kel'Thuzad was once of the Kirin Tor and once close enough to Antonidas's institutional world for the name to hit as a private and political failure at once.
Antonidas's stripped reaction on the stone confirms this is not a new enemy name but an old disaster returning through the plague.
Chapter 12 confirms that Kel'Thuzad still thinks in Antonidas's notation and still measures his own intellectual legitimacy against the teacher who expelled him.
Kel'Thuzad's version of Antonidas is not that he was ignorant, but that he lacked the will to carry sound theory into real bodies. That accusation becomes the relationship's defining wound.

trajectory: Scholarly peers and institutional rivals -> expulsion and absence -> chapter 8's return as the wound inside the sending-stone silence -> chapter 12's explicit split over what knowledge owes the body.
